sentences.text: Not--
--not in its true detail sense, no.
No, I don't think so.
I don't think so.
Now, from Arlo, as I said, end July, or I think it was end July, we were the, the battalion, which was [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH]-- 101 per 302.
That was the-- my battalion's name, 101 dash 302.
Well, look, I mean, there were hundreds of such battalions.
And that, that, you know, that identified that battalion.
So we went-- we came up to, to Budapest, which was very, very lucky.
We have been very lucky in our movements because we could have been just as easily taken out to the Ukraine, as a lot of other people have been.
But that was the luck.
And luck has a lot to do with one's survival.
I have learned that.
Oh, they have-- they have say in the day-to-day running of things, yes.
But not in the movements.

It was-- no, no, no, not even the-- not even the, the, the battalion leader, who was a lieutenant, you know, he would have been a, a reserve soldier who has been called in, who has been a, a, a country teacher, very quite a decent human being would, would-- that came out of the blue, and, you know.
And we moved.
So we came up to, to Pest, to Budapest.
And we have been installed in a building near [NON-ENGLISH].
[NON-ENGLISH] was a-- was a [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] rail [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] center where, you know, where, where supply came in, where supply have been reloaded and re-dispatched some other areas.
It was a rather important regional rail center.

And our task was to load and offload ammunition and other items into the wagons, and also to, to dismantle-- render harmless the time bombs, which have, by that time, been dropped by the Americans and the English.
And they didn't explode.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And the Americans and English, to a lesser degree, started to come up from Italy and started to bomb Hungary.
So that was a source of tremendous joy for us, right, because we realized that this is our final salvation.
Although the bombs didn't seek out Jewish or non-Jewish targets, and--
--it could have killed us just as easily as the, the time bombs-- which did not function, explode-- have killed some of our, our people there, right?
I haven't--
I have-- personally, I have not been involved in, in handling those time bombs.
But those who have been have not-- have been given five minutes training, I can assure you.
So they have been not really skilled in, in, in dealing with them.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]
So by that time, Hungary-- no, by that time, the provincial Jews, including Ujpest-- which was the last one on the 2nd of July-- have all been deported.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] In Hungary, with the return of, of the detached areas, it was about 800,000 Jews.
By that time, with the exception of approximately 200,000 Budapest Jews, they have all been deported.
They have been all in Auschwitz or other destination.
So that was the situation in, in, in August, 1944.
By that time, the Hungarian government started to see very clearly-- so much more than-- knew much more than we did-- that the war is lost by the Germans.
And they were starting to seek a, a way out.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] This, this doubt has permeated the general [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] populace.

And although the majority, the nontraditional Hungarians, who were themselves badly informed and subject to this, this misleading propaganda, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and of course, being wishful human beings themselves, they were all hoping that some miracle will happen.
By that time, the Germans started to send the the V-2s, to London.
And there was a lot of rumors circulating about the, the miracle weapons what the Germans are now developing.
So essentially they have been all hoping that these miracle weapons will, in, in this late stage, still work.
However, the official attitude showed a, a definite sign of appeasement.
We had the prime minister for a short time, who was Lakatos.
And he was the, the epitome of this-- of this realization that the war is lost.
And it's futile to, to, to, to, to, to, to fight.
And it's completely counterproductive to kill these 200,000 Jews now left in Budapest.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I-- I had an uncle-- I had relatives in Budapest.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I had-- I had my, my auntie, my uncle.
I had another uncle.
So I had quite a lot of people in Budapest.
My uncle, who was the black sheep of the family, who didn't want to be a lawyer, but became a baker-- remember I told you about him?
Yes.

He was a baker and a very clever man, very unusual man, who somehow-- I don't know how-- got some exception-- exemption for himself, right, based on his heroism during the First World War, all of which only, only 25% was true.
I know that.
But he got that.
And he somehow managed to, to, to hang onto his bakery in Budapest, which was a good one.
And he was exempted.
By that time in Budapest, Jews have been also moved into certain areas.
They were so-called protected houses.
Because by that time, the Swedish and the Swiss Embassy started some rescue activity.
You might have heard of Raoul Wallenberg.
He was one of them.
And we have-- they have effectively, by that time, established some very worthwhile rescue operation on international basis.

The Jewish internal resistance was still in the more localized Zionist front, right, who by that time, managed to get-- I imagine, I, I have no figures in mind, but I imagine, a good few hundred people through Romania out to Palestine.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]
I had-- I had-- I had the opportunity at that time-- I went to my, my, my battalion leader, to the lieutenant.
And I said that I would like to provide some special bread for the battalion.
And he allowed me to go every day to my uncle's bakery.
And I brought two big baskets full of goodies for him, right?
And that allowed-- that, that, that was very good.
And eventually, he allowed me to live outside.
So, for a few weeks, I had an outside [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] task to perform.
And I, I, I actually slept at my-- at my auntie's place, right?
And every morning, I went to my uncle.
I got the basket full of, of bread rolls.
And I took it to the-- back to the-- to the camp.
And I lived outside for a few weeks.

And that was, as, as I said to you, the time when there must have been a lot of soul-searching happening amongst the Hungarian leaders, which eventually culminated on the 15th of October by the so-called Horthy proclamation.
You know who Horthy was--
--the regent, the number one man in Hungary, who, by that time, realized that the war is well and truly lost.
And he read a proclamation into a-- into the radio declaring, declaring Hungary's, Hungary's neutrality, right, in the war.
I remember very crystal clear-- and it was on a Sunday.
And I was at my uncle's-- my baker uncle's-- house-- flat.
He lived quite luxuriously [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] measured by the times.
And we heard this proclamation.
And everybody was joyful.
And, well, that's the end of it.
And I walked to-- he lived on the second floor.
I walked to the window.
I saw the German soldiers on the street.
And I said to him-- his name was Ernie, Ernie, Ernie [NON-ENGLISH].
Ernie [NON-ENGLISH], that's not the end yet, the worst is yet to come.
He was very angry with me.
And he said that I'm-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] what is the best word in Hungarian to translate it?
[HUNGARIAN]-- I'm, I'm, I'm the-- I'm the-- I'm the carrier of, of bad news.
How would you say that in English?
We would say a doomsayer.
A doomsayer, yeah.
That's correct.
That's the right, right translation from [HUNGARIAN], which is in Hungarian.
And I said to him, well, I hope that I'm wrong, Ernie [NON-ENGLISH].
But unfortunately, I was right.
The Germans didn't take it lying down, as it was expected.

And the same night, the, the whole power was taken over by the extreme elements-- by Hungarian extreme elements, who were called-- not, not-- who have even a sign very similar to, to the swastika, right?
Is it the Arrow Cross?
Arrow Cross, yes--
--you know that, the Arrow Cross, right?
And that's when-- really, that was really the beginning of the end.
By that time, you must know that the Russians-- by the-- by August, Romania have defected.
The Balkans was, was virtually lost.
That's when the, the labor camp boys from Bor have been exterminated, pushing up from Yugoslavia.
The, the, the, the Eastern Front in Ukraine were very close.
In, in, in August-- in October, they have been already in Hungary, I would say.
They would have came through the straits.
And they were in Hungary by that time.
But, of course, we didn't know that in Budapest exactly.
And that's when these lunatics, lunatics took over.
And that's when the, the hell really broke loose.

Now we are entering into a period where, where intelligence, money, connections meant absolutely nothing, when luck and your ability to look into somebody's eye, to, to say a word with the right intonation is what counted.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I have decided-- I had a very difficult few days, because I knew that I am not going to go back to the labor camp.
They have been deported in a few days' time, as I learned later on.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I was-- I was rather-- I was rather helpless for a while.
I'm-- I wasn't sure what I was going to do.
I knew that this feeling of, of survival, you know, was getting stronger and stronger in me.
But I didn't know how I am going to go about it.
I remember-- could have been on the 16th or 17th of October, I knew that I'm not going to go back to my uncle either because that wasn't safe.
I went out to Ujpest, I remember, of course, no yellow star, no nothing.
I had some papers from the Zionists, which I got, that I was Horvath Laszlo.
That was my name.

And very coincidentally those papers were representing a, a, a non-Jewish boy of 17 years of age, younger than I, which was also very handy, because the non-Jewish 19-, 20-year-old boys have already been called in for military service, of course.

So I was-- I somehow got hold of these papers-- I'm not sure how, but from Zionist origin-- that I became Horvath Laszlo from Bekescsaba, where [NON-ENGLISH], my auntie who became a lawyer-- whom I showed, showed you, can show you here-- got married.
And I, as a child, spent some very pleasant summer holidays there.
So I knew the area very, very well.
So on the-- with this Horvath Laszlo paper, I went out to Ujpest.
And I, I remember, I-- on that night, I knocked on the door of my good Christian friend.
He died, meanwhile.
[NON-ENGLISH] was his name.
And it was night.
By that time, it was end of October, about 8, 9 o'clock at night.
And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I lived in [NON-ENGLISH].
And when I entered, they had people there.
They had just finished dinner.
And they realized, of course, straightaway that, that, you know, what predicament I am in.
They didn't ask too many questions.
They invited me in.
What was dangerous that other people were there.
But fortunately, they were decent people also.
So I joined them.
They fed me.
I was very hungry.
And, and we had a very neutral conversation.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] The other people left.
And then [NON-ENGLISH] asked me, oh, you might as well stay here tonight, right?
And they made a bed for me, and I stayed there that night.
And that could have saved my life because I managed to gather my thoughts, right?
And then I decided that the next day, I went up to the building in Budapest, which was the center of the Hungarian refugees.
Because by that time, Hungarian refugees were pouring in from the-- from the southern part as the Russians were pushing ahead.
And I presented myself as Laszlo Horvath, a 17-year-old boy from Bekescsaba.
And they asked me questions.
I answered the questions.
And they gave me-- the most important thing at that time was, was [PAUSES FOR 8 SECONDS] food coupons, right?
And, and I established-- I established an identity, right, as such.
I have a-- I have requisitioned a Jewish flat, people I knew.
And I knew that it was a-- was an abandoned flat, the address of what was 33 Merleg Street, Merleg utca. 'Merleg' is scale in Hungary.
And towards-- that was towards the end of October I moved into this, this flat.
The janitor in the flat-- I mean, in the flat was very few-- in the flat house was very few men because they have been, you know, they're-- no Jews were in the building.
The, the, the people-- non-Jewish people of military age have been called up.
They had it very tough.
I mean, they were hard times for everybody.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]
I was in this flat.
And after a few days, I realized that it's not really safe for me to stay in there because the janitor was the, the totum factum there.
You know what totum factum is?
Uh-huh.
He's the most important person, right?
The big potato there.
You know, I imagine, he started to wonder about Horvath Laszlo.
He's there all day.
So I went out and presented myself for work as a toolmaker in one of the still working factories there.
And I worked there for short-- part-time-- short time only.
Because, by that time, the Russians started to push in.
And Budapest, by that time, was surrounded.
And the factory was a fair while out.
I went there by streetcar.
And after two, three weeks, it was not possible to safely get there and get home.
From that moment on, of course, the janitor realized that, you know, I cannot go out.
And I was quite safe to stay in the house.

The Germans-- I mean, by that time, the-- to, to give you an idea, how I said to you earlier how important it is-- how important it is to, to look straight into somebody's eye in, in trying to salvage one's life.
On the-- one, one, one of the last days when I was coming back from work from this Hungarian factory, I had to walk from the streetcar to Merleg utca 33, where I lived.
And this factory had a library.
And I was taking home some books.
And as I was walking through-- it was a deserted street because it was already an open city.
It was not safe to walk on the streets.
You could hear cannons all over the place.
Planes were coming, either Russians or Germans, either Ratas or Stukas, right?
And it was-- it was war, in the crudest sense.
It was the very last few weeks.
And as I was walking through, I remember, I dropped one of these books.
And it sounded like an explosion.
So a German from one of the-- it was-- happened to be a German military [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] encampment there, on, on the-- on the-- on the-- on the piazza, on the-- on the square--
--right, where I was walking through.
And he jumped out, [SPEAKING GERMAN]!
So I went in.
And I said, I'm Horvath Laszlo.
I just came from work.
He didn't doubt me.
But he had no authority to let me go.
So I said, wait until the lieutenant, whoever, comes home-- comes back.
He wasn't there.
So I waited an hour.
It was getting late.
And eventually, he, he gave me a, a mattress.
Sleep here.
And, you know-- he's not here.
Probably he was visiting his girlfriend.
So next morning-- I slept very well.
I slept extra well, I remember.
In a German Army barracks?
In the army barracks.
And next morning, somebody kicks me up.
And the same man who has told me, well, he didn't turn up, doesn't matter.
You go to work, right?
So I went-- I collected my books, and I went.
I managed to play this role of Horvath Laszlo incredibly well.
Like an actor who plays the same role 500 times on the stage--
--he becomes that personality.
And I did become Horvath Laszlo.
No.
I didn't feel the tension either.
I did absolutely, retrospectively foolish things.
You know what I did?
My cousin was in one of the protected houses, [HUNGARIAN].
You might have heard about it.
It was a Glass House.
They call it the Glass House because it had a glass, glass wall.
And that's where hundreds of Jews have been gathering under the protection of Swiss [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] papers.

And my uncle, the little fellow you have seen on the-- the smallest, youngest boy, who, who was the-- who was a Zionist, he was an ardent Zionist way back in our family, who was the leader of these few hundred people hiding there.
And I knew that they are very hungry.
So I said to my uncle, the baker, give me few basket full of bread.
I take them in.
And he said, look, it's very dangerous.
They are surrounded by, by the Arrow Cross people.
Don't worry.
So I got a basketful.
I put on my leather coat.
That was my most precious worldly possession because leather was, was typical to power, right, military power, right?
I had a, a, a boot, a, a [HUNGARIAN], we called it, a leather boot, right?
And I had my other paraphernalia, my, my hat.
And my-- I put my Arrow Cross band on.
And I went into this what's the name of this house, right?
And when I got in, I told them who I was.
I took the band off.
I spoke to my cousin.
And, and I left the bread there.
And I walked out.
And nobody asked anything.
Nothing happened.
And they were-- if you want to be safest, you go to the lion's den.
That's the safest, right?
Anywhere else is considerably more dangerous.
And I did that.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] My uncle, my other uncle, has been taken away from another, another, another protected house.
My-- his daughter is here.
She's my double cousin, because two brothers married two sisters.
Very interesting situation.
[NON-ENGLISH] was his name.
And he was in a-- in another protected house.
And that was-- that would have been, oh, midway through third week in October where things were militarily not so exposed.
Because it was deteriorating day by day.
And I remember one day I went-- I went to visit them.
And, and [NON-ENGLISH] was in tears.
And [NON-ENGLISH], my cousin, was in tears.
No, my cousin was already in the-- in the-- in the Glass House.
That's where she was by that time.
And [NON-ENGLISH] told me that a few Arrow Cross people came in yesterday.
And they took 30 people away into the ghetto because there was a ghetto portion of Budapest at the time.
And [NON-ENGLISH] was amongst them.
So I said, don't worry.
We'll, we'll find him.
So I put on my paraphernalia again.
I walked into the ghetto with my-- they asked me.
I had to show my papers there.
I did.
And, and I made inquiries.
And I learned that yesterday, those few houses were the ones where new, new arrivals have been lodged.
So I went in.
It was a non-Jewish janitor in every house.
I told-- I showed, showed my credentials.
And I told them that there's a man by the name of Engel [NON-ENGLISH], [NON-ENGLISH] Engel, [NON-ENGLISH] Engel.
And we must find him because I have to deliver him to, to head office.
And I went back in half an hour.
And one of them has found, you know, because the records were kept.
And I went up, I remember, to the fourth floor.
A lift was there.
And [NON-ENGLISH] was shaving himself.
And he was looking into the glass.
And I walked in behind him, you know.
And [NON-ENGLISH] was not a particularly good, good actor.
And he turned around.
And he said something to the effect, [NON-ENGLISH]-- [LAUGHTER]
--what are you doing here?
[LAUGHS] So it was a very, [LAUGHS] very precarious situation.
Oh, dear.
How did you respond?
Anyhow--
--I got him.
I packed him up.
And I delivered him back to [NON-ENGLISH] through the gate of the ghetto.
Again, luck was with us.
Because it was-- by that time, was not very easy to just transport Jews, even if you had the Arrow Cross band on your arm.
But we just bluffed our way through.
And I managed to deliver him back to [NON-ENGLISH].
And that no doubt saved his life.
Yes, yes, look, I was not the only one.
There were many other Jews, fortunately.
Oh, visible?
Right, so I tell you what happened then.
So time is going on.
We are in, in November now, right?
Now, the, the-- even the Western escape routes for the Germans are, are narrowing by that time.
Everything is up in arm, everything, you know, a real emergency situation.

[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] The Jews who could not have been taken [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] by, by rail were marched to, to the-- to the west by-- on foot, right, which I heard from a lot of people-- some people who, who have done it, and who survived, it was a very painful, dangerous and-- event, during which-- during the march itself, they have been decimated.
But I did not take part of that.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] Those, those Jews who could not been taken by, by train or by foot were taken to the Danube and shot into the Danube.
That was another favorite way of, of getting rid of us.
Many, many hundreds, if not thousands, of people have died like that.
No, I haven't.
I haven't because I was Horvath Laszlo.
I was in the-- in the-- in the cellar of this house by that time.
Being the only man in the house, looking after the women and, and, and the girls, I did the heavy work.
And I was the hero there.
I was-- everybody-- you know.
To what extent, I tell you the last happening in that house, which was on the night of 18th of January.
Now we are in January, right, of 1945.
By that time, we realized that the Russians are very close.
We heard the, the loudspeakers in the vicinity.
It was a house-to-house fighting.
It was a horrible situation.
We were in, in the-- in the-- in the-- in the cellar downstairs.
Houses around us have been bombed to-- we couldn't recognize them.
And food was getting less, right, for the non-Jewish people.
And we were waiting for the-- for the end.
But even then, through misinformation and through, through wishful thinking, there was always, always hope in the-- in the non-Jewish population that a miracle will happen.
Same what the Jews thought, right?
It's in a human-- human trait.
And, and Merleg Street 33 was very close to the last bridge.
It was called the Lanchid, the [NON-ENGLISH], the, the Chain Bridge, which was the last link between Buda and Pest.
